turns.content: Hey, I'm contributing to a Rust open-source project for the first time. Can you explain how iterators work with `all` and `any`?

Sure! Think of an iterator like a lazy cursor moving through elements in a collection. When you call `.all()` or `.any()`, you're asking if all or any of those elements satisfy a condition, respectively.
Okay, so when I use `iter.all(|x| x > 0)`, what happens?

That checks if every single element in the iterator is greater than zero. If it encounters a number less than or equal to zero, it stops right away—this is short-circuit evaluation. It's like checking if all your kids are awake before leaving home; as soon as you see one sleeping, you don't need to check the rest.

That checks if there's at least one element that equals 42. If it finds the number 42, it stops and returns true without checking the rest—again, short-circuiting. This is like saying 'Is anyone in my office late?' If someone shows up early, you know no one is late.
That makes sense! So, what's the benefit of using these over a loop?

Great question! Using `.all()` and `.any()` is more idiomatic Rust because it leverages iterator methods. It avoids explicit loops and early returns, which can be error-prone. Also, they're lazy—elements are only accessed as needed. That means if your collection is large or infinite (like an `IntoIterator`), these functions won't waste resources by processing everything.
